stem: A 66-year-old client, 3 days post-hip replacement, suddenly develops acute dyspnea and chest pain. After reviewing the electronic health record, the nurse must complete the bowtie by selecting the most likely condition, two actions to take, and two parameters to monitor.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content:
Situation: Dr. Smith, this is Nurse Johnson on the orthopedic unit. I'm calling about Jane Doe in room 402, who is experiencing acute respiratory distress.
Background: She is a 66-year-old female, 3 days post-right total hip arthroplasty. Her risk factors include a BMI of 34, a history of smoking, and post-operative immobility.
Assessment: At 14:30, she suddenly developed pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. Her vitals are HR 118, RR 32, and SpO2 is 84% on room air. Her skin appears dusky.
Recommendation: I suspect a pulmonary embolism. I am applying oxygen and have placed her in high-Fowler's. I need you to come evaluate her immediately for potential anticoagulation and advanced imaging.

tabs.title: Vitals
tabs.content:
Time: 14:30
Blood Pressure: 132/86 mmHg
Heart Rate: 118/min
Respiratory Rate: 32/min
SpO2: 84% on room air
Note: Client is audibly gasping for air and appears anxious.

tabs.title: Provider Orders
tabs.content:
Active Orders:
  • Oxygen via non-rebreather mask at 15L/min
  • STAT Portable Chest X-ray
  • STAT D-dimer, CBC, CMP, PT/INR, aPTT
  • Prepare for STAT CT Angiography of the chest
  • Initiate Heparin IV infusion per VTE protocol for suspected PE

conditionOptions.hoverRationale: Incorrect. Hospital-acquired pneumonia typically presents with fever, productive cough, and lung consolidation sounds, which are absent. The onset is also usually more gradual than the sudden event described in the scenario.

Correct. The sudden onset of pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, hypoxia, and tachycardia in a post-operative, immobile client with multiple risk factors points to a pulmonary embolism. An embolus is obstructing pulmonary blood flow.

Incorrect. While atelectasis can cause dyspnea and low SpO2, it rarely causes the severe, sudden pleuritic chest pain and profound distress seen here. It is also typically associated with diminished breath sounds.

actionOptions.text: Elevate the head of the bed (HOB) to 45–90 degrees
actionOptions.hoverRationale: Correct. Placing the client in a high-Fowler's position uses gravity to facilitate lung expansion and decrease the work of breathing. This is a rapid, non-invasive intervention to improve oxygenation.

actionOptions.text: Initiate an IV Heparin infusion as prescribed
actionOptions.hoverRationale: Correct. For a suspected PE, anticoagulation with IV heparin is a priority to prevent further clot formation and propagation. This intervention directly addresses the underlying pathophysiology of the condition.

actionOptions.text: Encourage the client to ambulate to improve circulation
actionOptions.hoverRationale: Incorrect. Ambulation is contraindicated in a client with an acute, suspected PE. Movement could dislodge more of the thrombus from a DVT, worsening the embolism and causing further cardiorespiratory collapse.

actionOptions.text: Apply heat packs to the right calf for comfort
actionOptions.hoverRationale: Incorrect. Applying heat is not a priority and is irrelevant to managing this life-threatening respiratory emergency. The nurse's focus must be on stabilizing oxygenation and circulation.

parameterOptions.text: Continuous pulse oximetry and respiratory rate
parameterOptions.hoverRationale: Correct. Continuous monitoring of oxygen saturation and respiratory rate is essential to evaluate the client's response to oxygen therapy and other interventions, and to detect any further deterioration in respiratory status.
Correct. The activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) must be monitored frequently when a client is on a continuous IV heparin infusion to ensure the dose is therapeutic and prevent bleeding.
Incorrect. While important for general care, nutritional status and daily weight are not priority parameters for monitoring in the acute management of a life-threatening pulmonary embolism.

parameterOptions.text: Presence of a positive Babinski sign
parameterOptions.hoverRationale: Incorrect. The Babinski sign is a neurological assessment used to check for upper motor neuron lesions. It is completely unrelated to the pathophysiology or monitoring of a pulmonary embolism.
